description: Additive Spelio Welsh Foundations editorial content export.
notes: Additive content-only export for the revised Welsh Foundations collection.
Generated from docs/Spelio_Foundations_Curriculum.md, using the live database export schema shape as a compatibility reference.
Designed to be imported alongside existing live content without modifying app behaviour, database migrations, or admin fields.
Audio fields contain lightweight URL/status metadata only; physical audio files are not embedded.
Primer copy is stored in the top-level primerDrafts object and can be imported into list primer_content.
Welsh interface translations such as nameCy and descriptionCy are intentionally blank unless present in the source material.
REVIEW_NEEDED: WY/YW pronunciation grouping and examples require Welsh-speaker/audio validation before activation.

collections.name: Welsh Foundations
collections.description: A carefully designed path for building Welsh spelling confidence through listening, spelling, and pattern recognition.

lists.name: D / DD
lists.description: D and DD represent different sounds in Welsh. With practice, they quickly become easier to recognise.

words.usageNote: One of several ways to say “yes” in Welsh.

lists.name: Y
lists.description: Y behaves differently depending on where it appears in a word. This is one of the most useful Welsh spelling patterns to become familiar with.

words.usageNote: Notice how Y behaves differently here.
This word contains Y in two different positions.

lists.name: F / FF
lists.description: Welsh F and FF behave differently from English. Once this pattern becomes familiar, many Welsh words become much easier to spell.

words.usageNote: Notice how the single letter F sounds like the English V.
FF often sounds like a typical English F.

lists.name: W
lists.description: Unlike English, Welsh often uses W as part of the vowel sound. This pattern appears throughout the language.

words.usageNote: Notice how W behaves differently before a vowel.

lists.name: CH
lists.description: CH is one of the most recognisable Welsh spelling patterns. It is not pronounced like the CH in the English word “church”.

lists.name: LL
lists.description: LL is one of the most distinctive Welsh spelling patterns. Many Welsh place names and everyday words contain LL. With practice, it quickly becomes easier to recognise.

lists.name: RH
lists.description: RH is a common Welsh spelling pattern. Once familiar, it becomes easy to spot in everyday Welsh.

lists.name: AE / AI
lists.description: AI and AE appear in many common Welsh words. Although they are spelled differently, they often produce very similar sounds.

words.usageNote: AI often sounds a little like the English word “eye”.
AE often sounds similar to AI.

lists.name: WY
lists.description: WY appears in a number of common Welsh words. The exact sound can vary in some situations, but these examples provide a useful starting point.

lists.name: YW
lists.description: YW is usually much more consistent than WY. Once familiar, it becomes easy to recognise in Welsh words.

lists.name: OE
lists.description: OE is one of the more consistent Welsh vowel combinations. The same sound appears in many Welsh words.

lists.name: AU
lists.description: AU appears in a variety of Welsh words. Although it may look unfamiliar at first, the sound quickly becomes recognisable.

lists.name: AW
lists.description: AW is another common Welsh vowel pattern. Many learners find it easier than expected because it sounds quite familiar to English speakers.

lists.name: U
lists.description: Many learners expect U to behave like English U. In Welsh, it often sounds quite different. With practice, the pattern becomes familiar.

lists.name: C
lists.description: Unlike English, Welsh C does not change its sound. This makes Welsh spelling more predictable.

lists.name: G
lists.description: Welsh G behaves consistently. It does not switch between hard and soft sounds as it sometimes does in English.

lists.name: TH vs DD
lists.description: TH and DD are often confused by English speakers. Learning to hear the difference makes many Welsh words easier to recognise and spell.

words.usageNote: Notice how the sound changes compared with bodd.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence 1
lists.description: These words combine several patterns from earlier lessons. The goal is confidence, not perfection.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence 2
lists.description: Many of these words contain patterns you have already learned. Try noticing how those patterns become easier to recognise.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence 3
lists.description: By now you have seen many of the most important Welsh spelling patterns. The goal is to recognise them naturally when they appear together.

lists.name: Mixed Confidence 4
lists.description: These words are intended to feel slightly more natural and less controlled than the earlier lessons. Notice how many familiar patterns now appear automatically without needing to think about them.

editorialReference.introduction:
Welsh spelling is often more predictable than it first appears.
These short lessons introduce some of the most useful Welsh spelling patterns found in everyday Welsh.
Try to notice patterns rather than memorise rules.
Over time, these patterns begin to feel natural.
editorialReference.completionReflection:
You have now practised many of the most common Welsh spelling patterns.
You do not need to memorise every rule.
The aim of Foundations is to help Welsh spelling feel more familiar, more predictable, and less intimidating.
From here, the next stage is simply seeing these patterns appear again and again in useful Welsh words.

foundation_patterns_d_dd.primerBody:
D sounds like the D in the English word “den”.
DD sounds like the TH in the English word “then”.
foundation_patterns_d_dd.primerBodyCy:
Mae D yn swnio fel y D yn y gair Saesneg “den”.
Mae DD yn swnio fel TH yn y gair Saesneg “then”.
foundation_patterns_d_dd.teachingContext:
D and DD represent different sounds in Welsh.
With practice, they quickly become easier to recognise.

foundation_patterns_y.primerBody:
The letter Y can make different sounds in Welsh.
Earlier in a word it often sounds a little like the “u” in the English word “cut”.
In the final syllable of a word — and often in one-syllable words — it more commonly sounds like the “ee” in the English word “beet”.
foundation_patterns_y.primerBodyCy:
Gall y llythyren Y wneud gwahanol synau yn Gymraeg.
Yn gynharach mewn gair, mae'n aml yn swnio ychydig fel yr “u” yn y gair Saesneg “cut”.
Yn sillaf olaf gair — ac yn aml mewn geiriau un sillaf — mae'n fwy aml yn swnio fel “ee” yn y gair Saesneg “beet”.
foundation_patterns_y.teachingContext:
Y behaves differently depending on where it appears in a word.
This is one of the most useful Welsh spelling patterns to become familiar with.

foundation_patterns_f_ff.primerBody:
In Welsh:
* F often sounds like the English V.
* FF often sounds like the English F.
foundation_patterns_f_ff.primerBodyCy:
Yn Gymraeg:
* Mae F yn aml yn swnio fel V Saesneg.
* Mae FF yn aml yn swnio fel F Saesneg.
foundation_patterns_f_ff.teachingContext:
Welsh F and FF behave differently from English.
Once this pattern becomes familiar, many Welsh words become much easier to spell.

foundation_patterns_w.primerBody:
W often behaves like a vowel in Welsh.
It can sound a little like the “oo” in the English word “pool”.
When W comes before a vowel, it often sounds more like the W in the English word “wet”.
foundation_patterns_w.primerBodyCy:
Mae W yn aml yn ymddwyn fel llafariad yn Gymraeg.
Gall swnio ychydig fel “oo” yn y gair Saesneg “pool”.
Pan ddaw W cyn llafariad, mae'n aml yn swnio'n fwy fel y W yn y gair Saesneg “wet”.
foundation_patterns_w.teachingContext:
Unlike English, Welsh often uses W as part of the vowel sound.
This pattern appears throughout the language.

foundation_patterns_ch.primerBody:
CH is treated as a single Welsh sound.
It often sounds like the CH in the Scottish word “loch”.
foundation_patterns_ch.primerBodyCy:
Mae CH yn cael ei thrin fel un sain Gymraeg.
Mae'n aml yn swnio fel CH yn y gair Albanaidd “loch”.
foundation_patterns_ch.teachingContext:
CH is one of the most recognisable Welsh spelling patterns.
It is not pronounced like the CH in the English word “church”.

foundation_patterns_ll.primerBody:
LL is treated as its own Welsh sound.
It does not behave like two separate letters.
foundation_patterns_ll.primerBodyCy:
Mae LL yn cael ei thrin fel sain Gymraeg ei hun.
Nid yw'n ymddwyn fel dwy lythyren ar wahân.
foundation_patterns_ll.teachingContext:
LL is one of the most distinctive Welsh spelling patterns.
Many Welsh place names and everyday words contain LL.
With practice, it quickly becomes easier to recognise.

foundation_patterns_rh.primerBody:
RH is treated as its own Welsh sound.
It is often heard at the beginning of Welsh words.
foundation_patterns_rh.primerBodyCy:
Mae RH yn cael ei thrin fel sain Gymraeg ei hun.
Fe'i clywir yn aml ar ddechrau geiriau Cymraeg.
foundation_patterns_rh.teachingContext:
RH is a common Welsh spelling pattern.
Once familiar, it becomes easy to spot in everyday Welsh.

foundation_patterns_ae_ai.primerBody:
AI and AE often sound similar in Welsh.
They can sound a little like the English word “eye”.
foundation_patterns_ae_ai.primerBodyCy:
Mae AI ac AE yn aml yn swnio'n debyg yn Gymraeg.
Gallant swnio ychydig fel y gair Saesneg “eye”.
foundation_patterns_ae_ai.teachingContext:
AI and AE appear in many common Welsh words.
Although they are spelled differently, they often produce very similar sounds.

foundation_patterns_wy.primerBody: WY often sounds similar to the “oi” in the English word “coin”.
foundation_patterns_wy.primerBodyCy: Mae WY yn aml yn swnio'n debyg i “oi” yn y gair Saesneg “coin”.
foundation_patterns_wy.teachingContext:
WY appears in a number of common Welsh words.
The exact sound can vary in some situations, but these examples provide a useful starting point.

foundation_patterns_yw.primerBody: YW often sounds similar to the English word “you”.
foundation_patterns_yw.primerBodyCy: Mae YW yn aml yn swnio'n debyg i'r gair Saesneg “you”.
foundation_patterns_yw.teachingContext:
YW is usually much more consistent than WY.
Once familiar, it becomes easy to recognise in Welsh words.

foundation_patterns_oe.primerBody: OE sounds similar to the “oy” in the English word “boy”.
foundation_patterns_oe.primerBodyCy: Mae OE yn swnio'n debyg i “oy” yn y gair Saesneg “boy”.
foundation_patterns_oe.teachingContext:
OE is one of the more consistent Welsh vowel combinations.
The same sound appears in many Welsh words.

foundation_patterns_au.primerBody: AU often sounds similar to the long English “I” sound in words such as “sky” or “tie”.
foundation_patterns_au.primerBodyCy: Mae AU yn aml yn swnio'n debyg i sain hir “I” Saesneg mewn geiriau fel “sky” neu “tie”.
foundation_patterns_au.teachingContext:
AU appears in a variety of Welsh words.
Although it may look unfamiliar at first, the sound quickly becomes recognisable.

foundation_patterns_aw.primerBody: AW often sounds similar to the “ow” in the English word “cow”.
foundation_patterns_aw.primerBodyCy: Mae AW yn aml yn swnio'n debyg i “ow” yn y gair Saesneg “cow”.
foundation_patterns_aw.teachingContext:
AW is another common Welsh vowel pattern.
Many learners find it easier than expected because it sounds quite familiar to English speakers.

foundation_patterns_u.primerBody: In modern Welsh, U often sounds similar to I.
foundation_patterns_u.primerBodyCy: Yn y Gymraeg fodern, mae U yn aml yn swnio'n debyg i I.
foundation_patterns_u.teachingContext:
Many learners expect U to behave like English U.
In Welsh, it often sounds quite different.
With practice, the pattern becomes familiar.

foundation_patterns_c.primerBody:
Welsh C always keeps its hard sound.
It sounds like the C in the English word “cat”.
foundation_patterns_c.primerBodyCy:
Mae C Gymraeg bob amser yn cadw ei sain galed.
Mae'n swnio fel y C yn y gair Saesneg “cat”.
foundation_patterns_c.teachingContext:
Unlike English, Welsh C does not change its sound.
This makes Welsh spelling more predictable.

foundation_patterns_g.primerBody: Welsh G always keeps its hard sound.
foundation_patterns_g.primerBodyCy: Mae G Gymraeg bob amser yn cadw ei sain galed.
foundation_patterns_g.teachingContext:
Welsh G behaves consistently.
It does not switch between hard and soft sounds as it sometimes does in English.

foundation_patterns_th_vs_dd.primerBody:
DD sounds like the TH in the English word “then”.
TH sounds like the TH in the English word “think”.
foundation_patterns_th_vs_dd.primerBodyCy:
Mae DD yn swnio fel TH yn y gair Saesneg “then”.
Mae TH yn swnio fel TH yn y gair Saesneg “think”.
foundation_patterns_th_vs_dd.teachingContext:
TH and DD are often confused by English speakers.
Learning to hear the difference makes many Welsh words easier to recognise and spell.

foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_1.primerBody:
This review combines several patterns you have already practised.
There are no new rules here.
Simply notice how familiar patterns start working together.
foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_1.primerBodyCy:
Mae'r adolygiad hwn yn cyfuno sawl patrwm rydych eisoes wedi'u hymarfer.
Nid oes rheolau newydd yma.
Sylwch sut mae patrymau cyfarwydd yn dechrau gweithio gyda'i gilydd.
foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_1.teachingContext:
These words combine several patterns from earlier lessons.
The goal is confidence, not perfection.

foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_2.primerBody: This review introduces longer words and a wider mixture of familiar spelling patterns.
foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_2.primerBodyCy: Mae'r adolygiad hwn yn cyflwyno geiriau hirach a chymysgedd ehangach o batrymau sillafu cyfarwydd.
foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_2.teachingContext:
Many of these words contain patterns you have already learned.
Try noticing how those patterns become easier to recognise.

foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_3.primerBody: This review combines many of the patterns introduced throughout Foundations.
foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_3.primerBodyCy: Mae'r adolygiad hwn yn cyfuno llawer o'r patrymau a gyflwynwyd drwy gydol Foundations.
foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_3.teachingContext:
By now you have seen many of the most important Welsh spelling patterns.
The goal is to recognise them naturally when they appear together.

foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_4.primerBody:
These words introduce a broader range of familiar Welsh spelling patterns.
There are no new rules to learn.
Instead, use them as an opportunity to recognise patterns you have already practised.
foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_4.primerBodyCy:
Mae'r geiriau hyn yn cyflwyno ystod ehangach o batrymau sillafu Cymraeg cyfarwydd.
Nid oes rheolau newydd i'w dysgu.
Yn hytrach, defnyddiwch nhw fel cyfle i adnabod patrymau rydych eisoes wedi'u hymarfer.
foundation_patterns_mixed_confidence_4.teachingContext:
These words are intended to feel slightly more natural and less controlled than the earlier lessons.
Notice how many familiar patterns now appear automatically without needing to think about them.
